[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol)] Can an attacker take a sell offer with takerCallback = victimContract and receiver = attacker, causing safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victimContract, midnight, buyerAssets - sellerAssets) to fund the settlement fee and safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victimContract, attacker, sellerAssets) to send sellerAssets directly to the attacker, so the attacker receives sellerAssets of loanToken while victimContract pays buyerAssets? Preconditions: sell offer ratified by EcrecoverRatifier; victimContract has loanToken approval; attacker sets receiver = attacker, takerCallback = victimContract. Call sequence: (1) take(offer, ratifierData, units, attacker, attacker, victimContract, data); (2) payer = victimContract; (3) safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victimContract, midnight, buyerAssets - sellerAssets); (4) safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victimContract, attacker, sellerAssets). Invariant tested: receiver of sellerAssets must be funded by the legitimate payer (taker/msg.sender), not a third party; attacker must not receive loanToken at victim's expense. Scoped impact: direct theft of sellerAssets from victimContract to attacker. Proof idea: assert attacker.loanTokenBalance increases by sellerAssets while victimContract.loanTokenBalance decreases by buyerAssets.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: repay (Midnight.sol)] Can an attacker who has zero debt call repay(market, 0, attacker, victimCallback, data) with units = 0, causing onRepay to be called on victimCallback and safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victimCallback, midnight, 0) to execute, and determine whether this constitutes a no-op or whether the callback invocation itself causes harm (e.g., victimCallback performs state changes in onRepay that benefit the attacker)? Preconditions: attacker has zero debt; victimCallback implements IRepayCallback; units = 0. Call sequence: (1) repay(market, 0, attacker, victimCallback, data); (2) position[id][attacker].debt -= 0 (no-op); (3) onRepay(victimCallback) called with units = 0; (4) safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victimCallback, midnight, 0). Invariant tested: zero-unit repay with callback must not invoke callback with exploitable side effects; no-ops must be truly no-ops. Scoped impact: callback invocation abuse; potential state manipulation via zero-repay callback. Proof idea: test repay with units = 0 and victimCallback that has side effects; assert no state changes benefit attacker.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol) / EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an attacker create a sell offer with offer.group = sharedGroup and offer.maxAssets = largeAmount, sign it via EcrecoverRatifier, then take it with takerCallback = victimContract multiple times (each time draining victimContract for buyerAssets_i) until consumed[maker][sharedGroup] reaches maxAssets, achieving a total drain of sum(buyerAssets_i) from victimContract? Preconditions: sell offer with large maxAssets; victimContract has sufficient loanToken approval; attacker takes offer N times with takerCallback = victimContract. Call sequence: (1) for i in range(N): take(offer, ratifierData, units_i, attacker, attacker, victimContract, data); (2) each take drains victimContract for buyerAssets_i; (3) consumed[maker][group] increases until maxAssets. Invariant tested: offer consumption limits must not be circumventable via repeated payer confusion; victimContract's total loss must be bounded. Scoped impact: repeated drain of victimContract up to offer's maxAssets. Proof idea: fuzz N and units_i; assert sum(victimContract.loss_i) == sum(buyerAssets_i) and consumed[maker][group] <= maxAssets.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol)] Can an attacker take a sell offer at maturity boundary (block.timestamp == offer.market.maturity) with takerCallback = victimContract, where the sellerDebtIncrease == 0 check (CannotIncreaseDebtPostMaturity) passes because the seller has existing credit, but the payer confusion still drains victimContract for buyerAssets? Preconditions: block.timestamp == offer.market.maturity; seller has credit >= units (sellerDebtIncrease = 0); sell offer ratified by EcrecoverRatifier; takerCallback = victimContract. Call sequence: (1) warp to maturity; (2) take(offer, ratifierData, units, attacker, attacker, victimContract, data); (3) sellerDebtIncrease = 0, passes maturity check; (4) payer = victimContract; (5) victimContract drained. Invariant tested: maturity boundary must not create special conditions that amplify payer confusion; payer confusion must be testable at all timestamp boundaries. Scoped impact: payer confusion at maturity; victimContract drained. Proof idea: fuzz block.timestamp around maturity; assert payer confusion occurs regardless of timestamp.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol) / EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified] Can an attacker take a sell offer with takerCallback = victimContract where victimContract is a flash loan receiver that has temporarily received loanToken from Midnight.flashLoan(), causing victimContract to have a large loanToken balance at the time of the take, and the take drains that balance as payer before victimContract can repay the flash loan? Preconditions: victimContract is a flash loan receiver; attacker initiates flashLoan to victimContract; within onFlashLoan, attacker calls take with takerCallback = victimContract; victimContract has loanToken balance from flash loan. Call sequence: (1) flashLoan(tokens, assets, victimContract, data); (2) onFlashLoan: attacker calls take(offer, ..., victimContract, ...); (3) payer = victimContract; (4) safeTransferFrom drains victimContract's flash loan balance; (5) flashLoan repayment fails. Invariant tested: flash loan receiver's temporary balance must not be drainable via payer confusion; flash loan and take must not be composable to drain flash loan recipients. Scoped impact: flash loan recipient drained; flash loan repayment fails causing revert. Proof idea: compose flashLoan + take in single transaction; assert flash loan repayment fails if victimContract is drained.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: EcrecoverRatifier.isRatified / take (Midnight.sol)] Can an attacker submit ratifierData with a valid Merkle proof for offer A (with offer.callback = address(0)) but swap the offer struct to offer B (with offer.callback = victimContract) in the take() call, causing HashLib.hashOffer(offer) to produce a different hash that does not match the Merkle leaf, and confirm that isLeaf() correctly rejects this, or identify any hash collision or encoding ambiguity that allows the swap? Preconditions: attacker has valid proof for offer A; attacker modifies offer.callback to victimContract in the take() call. Call sequence: (1) take(offerB, ratifierDataForOfferA, units, taker, receiver, address(0), data); (2) isRatified: HashLib.hashOffer(offerB) != leaf in proof for offerA; (3) isLeaf returns false; (4) require(isLeaf(...), InvalidProof()) reverts. Invariant tested: offer hash must bind all fields including offer.callback; Merkle proof must not be reusable for modified offers. Scoped impact: confirm offer.callback binding in EcrecoverRatifier; identify any hash collision. Proof idea: differential test hashOffer(offerA) vs hashOffer(offerB) where only callback differs; assert they are different.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take (Midnight.sol)] Can an attacker take a sell offer with takerCallback = victimContract where victimContract is a contract that has approved Midnight for collateralToken (not loanToken), and determine whether the safeTransferFrom for loanToken still succeeds if victimContract has loanToken approval from a different source, or whether the wrong token approval causes a revert? Preconditions: victimContract has collateralToken.approve(midnight, max) but loanToken.approve(midnight, 0); sell offer ratified by EcrecoverRatifier. Call sequence: (1) take(offer, ..., victimContract, ...); (2) payer = victimContract; (3) safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victimContract, midnight, buyerAssets) fails due to insufficient loanToken approval. Invariant tested: payer confusion requires loanToken approval specifically; collateral token approval must not be sufficient. Scoped impact: confirm token-specific approval requirement for payer confusion. Proof idea: test victimContract with only collateralToken approval; assert take reverts; test with loanToken approval; assert take succeeds and drains victimContract.
